focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

June had texted that they'd figure it out. By the next morning, with rain pasting free-pizza flyers flat to the South Philadelphia sidewalk, Lena took that to mean June's car keys. She went down June's basement steps with her phone in one hand and Eli's last message open on the screen long enough for the rain to dot it silver.

June's place always felt like the underside of the block: cooler, dimmer, full of cords and old machine heat. A fan buzzed in one corner for no reason Lena could identify except habit. Mateo was at the folding table in a gray Central hoodie with his beat-up headphones around his neck, picking a gummy bear off the W key of a school Chromebook with the edge of a library card. June stood at the counter in work boots and black socks, one lace trailing, tearing open junk mail with the nicotine-yellowed side of her thumbnail.

She looked up once. "You look bad."

"Thanks."

"You want coffee or the truth."

"I want your car keys."

Mateo stopped worrying the gummy bear and looked between them without moving anything else in his face.

June laughed, quick and automatic. "For what."

Lena held the phone out. Eli's message sat there in the same patient cadence that had kept working on her even after it started to chill her. "Long wet load-in. Harborlight's floor is murdering my knees. If I survive tech, I'm stealing you Sunday." Below it was the earlier one from Tuesday with the too-specific little detail that had done the damage: "The space is this old annex off Ocean. Smells like dust and salt and somebody's bad coffee." He had sent a picture after that, a black dance floor rolled halfway out over scuffed wood, cinderblock painted cream, one red EXIT sign.

"I'm going down there," Lena said. "Wildwood, Crest, wherever the hell this is suppo...
prev_tail_excerpt:
"Better," he said.

"A little."

"Good. Stay with me there."

Cool air moved through the room. His next line came right after it, neat as if the opening had been waiting.

He said, "You know what I love about you," and her body went ahead of him, braced for the inventory: her work hands, the way she listened, the mean little joke she made when she needed reassurance without asking for it. He'd hand it back polished and warm enough to wear.

"What," she said.

"That even when you're wrecked, you're still trying to make the story accurate."

It was a beautiful answer. It was even true.

She rolled onto her side and pressed her forehead to the cool wall. "I should sleep."

He did not push. "Okay."

"Okay?"

"Yeah." Softly: "You don't have to hand me the clean-copy version tonight."

The phrase nicked the same spot behind her ribs.

"Call me tomorrow?" she said.

"Of course."

The answer was there too fast, like he'd had it loaded before she asked.

After they said goodnight she left the phone on the pillow and did not move for a long time. The apartment had gone cold in damp increments. On the desk, the tax envelope leaned under the lamp. Her wet cuffs from earlier still hung over the chair. Somewhere down on Broad a bus hit standing water and sent up the long shhhk of a city being dragged through weather.

Eli had made her feel better. That was still true. But afterward the room felt damp with him, his voice in the lampshade, the tax envelope, the wet cuffs sagging over the chair. Not a door opening. More like humidity getting into paper and wood while the rain kept worrying the walls.
